question: A patient presents with acute abdominal pain and signs of peritonitis. Cross-sectional abdominal imaging has been obtained. Based on this image and the clinical scenario, which operative approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Emergency laparotomy with segmental colon resection and end colostomy (Hartmann)
options.B: Emergency laparotomy with segmental resection and primary anastomosis
options.C: Laparoscopic peritoneal lavage and drainage only
options.D: Nonoperative management with IV antibiotics and observation
options.E: Endoscopic stent placement across the affected bowel

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the coronal CT image for acute findings. I see multiple small collections of extraluminal gas (indicated by the arrows) tracking adjacent to the lower abdominal/left pelvic bowel, with surrounding inflammatory stranding and fluid density in the peritoneal cavity. These findings are radiologic evidence of bowel perforation with peritoneal contamination.

Next, I relate those imaging findings to the clinical stem, which reports acute abdominal pain and signs of peritonitis. The combination of free/extraluminal intraperitoneal air and inflammatory change on CT explains the peritonitic exam and indicates a surgical source control problem rather than a purely medical one.

Then I consider the operative options in light of source control and contaminated/unprepared bowel. Nonoperative management (IV antibiotics/observation) and endoscopic stent placement do not address free perforation with peritoneal contamination. Laparoscopic peritoneal lavage alone would not definitively remove the perforated segment or control ongoing contamination when there is a clear adjacent perforation.

Finally, between resection with primary anastomosis versus resection with end colostomy, the safest option in the setting of active peritonitis and an unprepared, contaminated field is to avoid a fresh anastomosis. Therefore, performing an emergency laparotomy with segmental colon resection and end colostomy (Hartmann procedure) best addresses source control while minimizing anastomotic risk.

Because the image shows extraluminal air and peritoneal contamination consistent with a perforated colon and peritonitis, the most appropriate operative approach is resection with end colostomy.
caption: Abdominal computed tomography (coronal view) demonstrating a large metastatic tumor occupying a significant part of the liver parenchyma, and multiple tumors in a number of loops of the small intestine (arrows) infiltrating the bowel wall.
sub_caption: Abdominal computed tomography (coronal view) demonstrating a large metastatic tumor occupying a significant part of the liver parenchyma, and multiple tumors in a number of loops of the small intestine (arrows) infiltrating the bowel wall.
image_context: An 81-year-old woman was admitted to the emergency department of our institution due to fatigue and malaise. Upon admission the patient was hemodynamically stable. The patient's history of atrial fibrillation was significant and a Coumadin analog was administered. Additionally, the patient had a history of a locally advanced non-small cell carcinoma of the right lung (stage IIIB); her lesion was considered unresectable at the time and the patient received cisplatin and etoposide combined with radiotherapy, showing a significant response. Approximately 1.5 years later, she was diagnosed with a metastatic lesion of the left lung during follow-up. Chemotherapy was restarted, but the patient was lost to follow-up in the next year. The patient reported mild abdominal discomfort, which had started 2 weeks prior to the current presentation. Upon admission, she presented with severe anemia (Hct 18.5%), leucopenia (WBC 2,330 K/µl), thrombocytopenia (PLT 55,000 K/µl), and a protracted INR of 6.41. Chest X-ray showed diffuse pulmonary infiltrations and lobular pneumonia at the right lower lobe. The abdominal ultrasound showed multiple focal lesions in both hepatic lobes, with features of metastasis. At 2 days post-admission, the patient presented with diffuse abdominal pain combined with fever (38.1°C), and surgical consultation was demanded. At physical examination the patient had diffuse abdominal tenderness and guarding, rebound tenderness, abdominal distention and obstipation. Her blood test revealed Hct 22.1%, WBC 20,000 K/µl, PLT 47,000 K/µl, whereas the biochemical tests were within normal limits. An abdominal CT revealed wall thickening at the level of the mid-sigmoid and the presence of free air in the peritoneal cavity, likely due to perforation of the gastrointestinal tract. Several round lesions were infiltrating the wall of the small bowel, 2–3 cm in diameter, and large metastatic lesions were also revealed within the liver parenchyma. A moderate quantity of ascitic fluid, and multiple enlarged lymph nodes within the mesentery were also identified (<xref rid="ol-17-04-3862-g00" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>). The patient was urgently led to surgery for an exploratory laparotomy. Upon entering the peritoneal cavity, diffuse feculent peritonitis, due to perforation of the jejunum 1 m from the ligament of Treitz, was found. In addition, six distinct tumors of the jejunum close to the perforation site were found, as well as two more similar lesions caudally at the ileum (). The patient was urgently led to surgery for an exploratory laparotomy. Upon entering the peritoneal cavity, diffuse feculent peritonitis, due to perforation of the jejunum 1 m from the ligament of Treitz, was found. In addition, six distinct tumors of the jejunum close to the perforation site were found, as well as two more similar lesions caudally at the ileum (<xref rid="ol-17-04-3862-g01" ref-type="fig">Fig. 2A</xref>). The tumors were round, hard and protruding from the bowel wall, with dimensions <4 cm. A separate stenotic tumor at the wall of the mid-sigmoid was identified, which caused moderate dilation of the proximal intestine (). The tumors were round, hard and protruding from the bowel wall, with dimensions <4 cm. A separate stenotic tumor at the wall of the mid-sigmoid was identified, which caused moderate dilation of the proximal intestine (<xref rid="ol-17-04-3862-g01" ref-type="fig">Fig. 2B</xref>). Enterectomy, including the perforation site and the proximal six tumors, was first performed, followed by limited enterectomies for the distal two tumors (). Enterectomy, including the perforation site and the proximal six tumors, was first performed, followed by limited enterectomies for the distal two tumors (<xref rid="ol-17-04-3862-g01" ref-type="fig">Fig. 2C and D</xref>). The continuity of the gastrointestinal tract was reinstated with side-to-side anastomoses of the small intestine using a linear stapler. For the sigmoid tumor, a Hartmann procedure with limited resection of the mid-sigmoid was performed. The patient remained in a severe septic condition following surgery and succumbed the next day.). The continuity of the gastrointestinal tract was reinstated with side-to-side anastomoses of the small intestine using a linear stapler. For the sigmoid tumor, a Hartmann procedure with limited resection of the mid-sigmoid was performed. The patient remained in a severe septic condition following surgery and succumbed the next day.
relevant_image_context: An abdominal CT revealed wall thickening at the level of the mid-sigmoid and the presence of free air in the peritoneal cavity, likely due to perforation of the gastrointestinal tract. Several round lesions were infiltrating the wall of the small bowel, 2–3 cm in diameter, and large metastatic lesions were also revealed within the liver parenchyma.
